perturbation.original_text: If any Party discovers (a) any material breach of the Security Regulations or of the systems used to provide the Services or (b) any breach or threatened breach of the Security Regulations that involves or may reasonably be expected to involve unauthorized access, disclosure or use of the other Party's or its Affiliates' Confidential Information (each of (a) and (b), a "Security Incident"), such Party shall, at the cost of the Party responsible for the Security Incident, (i) promptly (both orally, if practicable, and in any event in writing) notify the other Party of the Security Incident and (ii) reasonably cooperate with the other Party (1) to take commercially reasonable measures necessary to control and contain the security of such Confidential Information, (2) to remedy any such Security Incident, including using commercially reasonable efforts to identify and address any root causes for such Security Incident, (3) to furnish full details of the Security Incident to the other Party and keep such other Party advised of all material measures taken and other developments with respect to such Security Incident, (4) in any litigation or formal action with third parties or in connection with any regulatory, investigatory or other action of any Governmental Authority and (5) in notifying the other Party's or its Affiliates' customers and Personnel and other persons of the Security Incident to the extent reasonably requested by the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: If any Party discovers (a) any material breach of the Security Regulations or of the systems used to provide the Services or (b) any breach or threatened breach of the Security Regulations that involves or may reasonably be expected to involve unauthorized access, disclosure or use of the other Party's or its Affiliates' Confidential Information (each of (a) and (b), a "Security Incident"), such Party may, at its sole discretion, notify the other Party of the Security Incident. The discovering party is not obligated to cooperate with the other Party to take any measures to control, remedy, or furnish details of such Security Incident.
perturbation.explanation: The original Section 4.1 defines 'Security Incident' and mandates a specific response protocol, including prompt notification and reasonable cooperation. The modified text introduces optionality ('may' instead of 'shall') regarding notification and removes the obligation to cooperate. This creates a contradiction because the original text clearly states the required actions upon discovering a security incident, while the modified version grants discretion to the discovering party, effectively negating the mandatory nature of the original obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 4.1 Security Breaches. If any Party discovers (a) any material breach of the Security Regulations or of the systems used to provide the Services or (b) any breach or threatened breach of the Security Regulations that involves or may reasonably be expected to involve unauthorized access, disclosure or use of the other Party's or its Affiliates' Confidential Information (each of (a) and (b), a "Security Incident"), such Party may, at its sole discretion, notify the other Party of the Security Incident. The discovering party is not obligated to cooperate with the other Party to take any measures to control, remedy, or furnish details of such Security Incident.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 4.1 mandates notification and cooperation in the event of a security incident. The changed text makes notification discretionary and removes the obligation to cooperate. This directly contradicts the original mandatory requirements, creating a clear inconsistency within the contract regarding the response to security incidents, leading to uncertainty about the parties' obligations.

perturbation.original_text: RGHI will not be in in breach of this Agreement if RGHI declines to provide a requested additional service for any good faith reason, including the failure of the Parties to agree to the scope, term, and fee for the additional service.
perturbation.changed_text: RGHI will be in breach of this Agreement if RGHI declines to provide a requested additional service, regardless of reason.
perturbation.explanation: The original Section 2.1(a) states that RGHI will not breach the agreement if they decline to provide additional services for any good faith reason. The change now states that RGHI will breach if they decline regardless of reason. This now directly contradicts the rest of section 2.1(a).
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 2.1 Services. (a) During the applicable Term of any Service, and in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement, RGHI shall provide, or shall cause its Affiliates or, subject to Section 2.2, third parties to provide, to the Company or one or more of its Affiliates (in connection with the conduct of the Business) the services described on Exhibit A hereto (the "Transition Services"). Notwithstanding the content of Exhibit A, RGHI agrees to consider in good faith any reasonable request by the Company for access to any additional service that is necessary for the operation of the Business, at fees to be agreed upon after good faith negotiation between the parties. RGHI will be in breach of this Agreement if RGHI declines to provide a requested additional service, regardless of reason. Any such additional services so provided by RGHI shall constitute Services hereunder and be subject in all respects to the provisions of this Agreement as if fully set forth on Exhibit A as of the date hereof.
perturbation.justification: The changed text stating that RGHI will be in breach if they decline to provide a requested additional service, regardless of reason, directly contradicts the original provision in Section 2.1(a). The original text stated that RGHI would *not* be in breach if they declined for any good faith reason. The change eliminates the 'good faith reason' exception, creating a direct and irreconcilable contradiction within Section 2.1(a) itself. This falls under Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction, as the clause is placed in multiple locations with conflicting implications (the original text and the changed text).
